title: Guard-book binding, a structure for loose inserts
subtitle: How to construct a binding that accepts removable loose sheets
excerpt: A guard book has no fixed pages. Instead, paper guards, narrow folded strips, are sewn together to form a text block. Each guard accepts one loose sheet that can be inserted, removed, or rearranged. The structure suits stamp collections, pressed botanical specimens, and any archive where the contents change over time.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on mounting, guards, and the care of maps: guard construction described in detail with guidance on guard width relative to paper thickness. Cyril Davenport, The Book: Its History and Development (1907), background on the structure's archival use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A narrow folded strip of paper or card sewn into a binding to provide a hinge or to accept a loose insert.
The increase in spine thickness caused by sewing. Deliberate swell in a guard book is desirable because it creates the gap that loose inserts occupy.

content.text: Guard-book binding was the standard archival structure for Victorian natural-history collections and postage-stamp albums before ring binders existed. Each

is a folded strip of thin but strong paper, roughly 15-20 mm wide when folded. The sewn text block is all guards; the inserts ride in the pockets between them. A finished guard book opens completely flat because the sewing follows the fold of each guard, not through a page.

attrs.title: Bookbinding adhesives safety
attrs.body: PVA and wheat-paste are safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes with warm water immediately after use. PVA dries irreversibly, work in small amounts and cap the bottle between uses.

items.name: Guard paper
items.notes: Thin, strong paper at 60-80 gsm. Cartridge paper is too heavy, it prevents the book from opening flat. Japanese tissue or a thin kozo paper is ideal for archival work; thin photocopier paper serves adequately at practice scale.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat, A3

content.text: Cut strips of guard paper to the height of the book and roughly 30-40 mm wide. Fold each strip in half lengthways across the grain direction, the fold should run parallel to the short dimension of the paper, not against it. Bone the fold firmly. Stack all the folded guards in sets of four to six; each set becomes one signature of guards.

from sewing guard sets is what opens space for the inserts. A book designed to hold inserts of 120 gsm paper at 5 inserts per guard-set needs a swell equal to 5 × 0.12 mm at the spine. Design the guard width accordingly, wider guards swell less; narrower guards swell more. Too little swell and the inserts buckle; too much and the book closes at an angle.

Pierce sewing stations through each set of guards using the awl. Three or four stations suffice for books up to 200 mm tall. Sew the sets together with a simple link stitch, working a kettle stitch at each end.
